sections.title: Hedge Funds - Strategies and Fees
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Hedge funds are pooled investment vehicles that use a WIDE RANGE of strategies to earn returns. They're called 'hedge' funds because they were originally designed to HEDGE (reduce) risk, but modern hedge funds often take SIGNIFICANT risks.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE EXAM: Alternative Investments represents 5-8% of the CFA Level I exam (approximately 9-14 questions). Hedge funds are the MOST TESTED alternative investment.

HEDGE FUND CHARACTERISTICS:

  • Private pools: Available only to wealthy/qualified investors
  • Less regulated: More flexibility than mutual funds
  • Can use leverage: Borrow money to amplify returns (and risks!)
  • Can short sell: Profit from declining prices
  • Can use derivatives: Options, futures, swaps for hedging or speculation
  • Illiquid: Lock-up periods (can't withdraw for months/years)

HEDGE FUND FEE STRUCTURE - THE '2 AND 20':

Fee TypeTypical AmountBased On
Management Fee2% per yearAssets Under Management (AUM)
Incentive Fee20% of profitsInvestment gains

EXAMPLE: You invest $1 million in a hedge fund. The fund earns 15% return ($150,000 profit).

  • Management Fee: 2% × $1,000,000 = $20,000
  • Incentive Fee: 20% × $150,000 = $30,000
  • Total Fees: $50,000
  • Your Net Return: $150,000 - $50,000 = $100,000 (10%)

Even though the fund earned 15%, you only received 10% after fees!

COMMON HEDGE FUND STRATEGIES:

  1. Long/Short Equity:
    • Buy (go long) undervalued stocks
    • Sell short overvalued stocks
    • Profit from BOTH rising and falling prices
    • EXAMPLE: Long Ford (think undervalued), Short Tesla (think overvalued)
  2. Global Macro:
    • Bet on MACROECONOMIC trends
    • Trade currencies, interest rates, commodities, stock indices
    • EXAMPLE: Bet that US dollar will strengthen vs. Euro
    • Famous example: George Soros 'broke the Bank of England' by shorting the British pound
  3. Event-Driven:
    • Profit from CORPORATE EVENTS
    • Mergers, acquisitions, bankruptcies, restructurings
    • EXAMPLE: Company A announces acquisition of Company B at $50/share. Current price is $48. Hedge fund buys at $48, bets deal will close at $50.
  4. Relative Value:
    • Exploit PRICE DIFFERENCES between related securities
    • EXAMPLE: Same stock trades at $100 on NYSE and $100.50 on LSE. Buy on NYSE, sell on LSE.

HEDGE FUND RISKS:

  • Leverage Risk: Borrowing amplifies losses as well as gains
  • Liquidity Risk: Can't withdraw during lock-up periods
  • Manager Risk: Performance depends heavily on manager skill
  • Transparency Risk: Hedge funds don't disclose holdings (unlike mutual funds)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember '2 and 20' - 2% management fee, 20% incentive fee. Also remember 'LSGER' - Long/Short, Global Macro, Event-Driven, Relative Value (the four main strategies).
keyPoints: Alternatives = 5-8% of exam (9-14 questions)
Hedge funds = Private, less regulated, flexible strategies
Management Fee = 2% of AUM (typical)
Incentive Fee = 20% of profits (typical)
'2 and 20' = Standard hedge fund fee structure
Long/Short Equity = Buy undervalued, short overvalued
Global Macro = Bet on economic trends (currencies, rates)
Event-Driven = Profit from mergers, bankruptcies
Relative Value = Exploit price differences
Hedge funds can use leverage, short selling, derivatives
Lock-up periods = Can't withdraw for specified time
Memory aid: '2 and 20' fees, 'LSGER' strategies

formulas.expression: Net Return = Gross Return - Management Fee - Incentive Fee

practiceQuestions.question: A hedge fund charges '2 and 20'. If the fund has $100 million AUM and earns 15% return, what is the TOTAL fee?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Management Fee = 2% × $100M = $2M. Profit = 15% × $100M = $15M. Incentive Fee = 20% × $15M = $3M. Total Fees = $2M + $3M = $5M. EXAM TIP: Remember to calculate BOTH fees and ADD them. The '2 and 20' structure can significantly reduce investor returns!

practiceQuestions.question: A hedge fund that bets on merger announcements is using which strategy?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Event-Driven strategies profit from CORPORATE EVENTS like mergers, acquisitions, bankruptcies, and restructurings. A merger arbitrage fund buys the target company's stock after a deal is announced, betting the deal will close. EXAM TIP: Remember the strategies: Long/Short = Stock picking, Global Macro = Economic trends, Event-Driven = Corporate events, Relative Value = Price differences.

sections.title: Private Equity and Real Estate
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Private equity involves investing in PRIVATE COMPANIES (not publicly traded). Real estate involves investing in PROPERTY. Both are major alternative investment categories.

WHY THIS MATTERS: Private equity and real estate are COMMON exam topics within alternatives. Know the strategies and characteristics.

PRIVATE EQUITY STRATEGIES:

  1. Venture Capital:
    • Invest in EARLY-STAGE companies (startups)
    • HIGH RISK, HIGH POTENTIAL RETURN
    • EXAMPLE: Investing in a tech startup before it goes public
    • Stages: Seed (idea stage) → Early stage → Later stage → Mezzanine (pre-IPO)
    • Most startups FAIL, but successful ones can return 10-100× the investment
  2. Leveraged Buyouts (LBOs):
    • Buy MATURE companies using mostly DEBT (leverage)
    • Improve operations, pay down debt, sell or IPO
    • EXAMPLE: Private equity firm buys Company X for $1B, puts in $300M equity, borrows $700M. Uses Company X's cash flows to pay down debt. Sells for $2B in 5 years.
    • Target companies have: Stable cash flows, undervalued assets, room for improvement
  3. Growth Capital:
    • Invest in MATURE companies seeking to EXPAND
    • Less risky than venture capital
    • EXAMPLE: Company wants to enter new markets, needs capital for expansion

PRIVATE EQUITY CHARACTERISTICS:

  • Illiquid: 5-10 year lock-up periods
  • J-Curve Effect: Early years show NEGATIVE returns (fees, setup costs), later years show POSITIVE returns (exits)
  • High fees: Similar to hedge funds (2 and 20)
  • Active management: PE firms actively improve portfolio companies

REAL ESTATE INVESTMENT:

WAYS TO INVEST:

  1. Direct Ownership: Buy property directly (apartments, offices, retail)
  2. REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts): Publicly traded companies that own real estate. Trade like stocks, provide liquidity.
  3. Real Estate Funds: Pooled investments in multiple properties

REAL ESTATE VALUATION:

Cap Rate = Net Operating Income (NOI) / Property Value

EXAMPLE: An apartment building generates $100,000 annual NOI and is valued at $1,250,000.
Cap Rate = $100,000 / $1,250,000 = 8%

INTERPRETATION: Higher cap rate = HIGHER return but HIGHER risk. Lower cap rate = LOWER return but LOWER risk.

REAL ESTATE CHARACTERISTICS:

  • Inflation Hedge: Rents and property values tend to rise with inflation
  • Income Generation: Rental income provides steady cash flow
  • Diversification: Low correlation with stocks and bonds
  • Illiquid: Takes time to buy/sell properties
  • High Transaction Costs: Legal fees, inspections, closing costs

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'VLG' - Venture Capital, LBOs, Growth Capital (the three PE strategies). Also remember 'Cap Rate = NOI / Value' - like a dividend yield for real estate. Higher cap rate = higher risk/return.
keyPoints: Venture Capital = Early-stage, high risk/high return
LBO = Buy mature companies with high DEBT (leverage)
Growth Capital = Mature companies expanding
J-Curve Effect = Early negative returns, later positive
Private equity = 5-10 year lock-up (illiquid)
REITs = Publicly traded real estate (liquid)
Cap Rate = NOI / Property Value
Higher cap rate = Higher return but higher risk
Real estate = Inflation hedge, income generation
Direct ownership vs. REITs vs. Real estate funds
Memory aid: 'VLG' - Venture, LBO, Growth (PE strategies)

formulas.expression: Cap Rate = Net Operating Income (NOI) / Property Value

practiceQuestions.question: Venture capital investments are BEST described as:
practiceQuestions.explanation: Venture capital invests in EARLY-STAGE companies (startups) with HIGH RISK but HIGH POTENTIAL RETURNS. Most startups fail, but successful ones can return 10-100× the investment. EXAM TIP: Remember the PE spectrum: Venture Capital (earliest, riskiest) → Growth Capital → LBOs (latest, least risky).

practiceQuestions.question: A property generates $200,000 in net operating income and is valued at $2,500,000. What is the cap rate?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Cap Rate = NOI / Property Value = $200,000 / $2,500,000 = 0.08 = 8%. This means the property generates an 8% return based on its current value. EXAM TIP: Cap rate is like a 'dividend yield' for real estate. It's the inverse of a P/E ratio. Higher cap rate = higher return but higher risk.
